channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Schumer and Platner Talk After Mills Suspends Her Campaign
item.description: The top Senate Democrat and the Maine contender had what was described as a cordial conversation despite Schumer’s backing of his primary opponent.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate in Maine’s Senate race, speaking at a town hall in Sabattus, Maine, on Wednesday.

item.title: Takeaways From Hegseth’s Second Day of Testimony on the Iran War
item.description: The secretary was also questioned over civilian deaths, an accusation of antisemitic remarks and women in combat.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, left, and Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, testifying before the Senate Armed Services Committee on Thursday.

item.title: Trump’s Plans to Boost Weapons Production Might Not Deliver for Years
item.description: While the defense industry has announced plans to make more munitions, much of that expanded production will not quickly kick in.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The administration does not yet have the funding or congressional support for the weapons it needs for the possible future wars.

item.title: Comey Indictment Shows Justice Dept. Got the Message From Bondi’s Firing
item.description: In naming only an interim successor as acting attorney general, President Trump has established even greater incentives to execute his most extreme demands, current and former officials say.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, who is seeking to become the permanent attorney general, is currently examining a range of other potential charges against James B. Comey.

item.title: Last on King Charles’s U.S. Tour: A Potluck and a Win for Scottish Whisky
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla had a ceremonial farewell at the White House before attending a block party in Front Royal, Va.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The president and the first lady bidding farewell to King Charles III and Queen Camilla on Thursday.

item.title: Senate Bans Prediction Markets for Its Members and Staff
item.description: Senators voted unanimously to prohibit betting practices in the chamber after some users made hundreds of thousands of dollars online by accurately predicting U.S. military actions.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anonymous users of prediction markets like Kalshi can bet on a certain outcome of future events, including major policy decisions of the U.S. government.

item.title: Congress Passes Another Short-Term Extension of Expiring Surveillance Law
item.description: Senator Ron Wyden secured an agreement to seek the declassification of a recent intelligence court ruling about the FISA Section 702 program.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage and Michael Gold
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: What Peak Gerrymandering Could Look Like Now
item.description: The redrawing of America’s congressional districts is sure to escalate after the Supreme Court’s decision, with some maps that would have seemed laughable a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Supreme Court Voting Rights Ruling Could Fuel New Era of Redistricting Wars
item.description: The expected flood of new congressional maps is likely to produce fewer competitive districts, fewer ways for voters to hold elected officials accountable and more polarized politics.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on the Voting Rights Act could unleash another redistricting arms race.

item.title: Hegseth Says Iran Cease-Fire Stops Clock for Congressional Approval
item.description: The defense secretary testified on the eve of the 60-day mark of the war, a major statutory deadline for the president to withdraw forces or seek approval from Congress to continue the fight.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified for the second straight day on Capitol Hill on Thursday.

item.title: Mills Exit Is a Blow to Schumer as Democrats Question His Strategy
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer, the New York Democrat and minority leader, considered the Maine governor his top recruit for winning the majority. Critics said her collapse showed he is out of touch with the party’s voters.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer, Democrat of New York and the minority leader, at the Capitol last month.

item.title: Trump Picks Nicole Saphier for Surgeon General After Withdrawing Casey Means Nomination
item.description: Dr. Means’s nomination had stalled in part over her views on vaccines. The president said he was instead nominating Dr. Nicole B. Saphier, a radiologist at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg and Dani Blum
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times
item.media:description: The withdrawal of Dr. Casey Means’s nomination is bound to be a deep disappointment for the Make America Healthy Again movement.

item.title: House Passes DHS Funding Bill, Ending Shutdown
item.description: Republicans were forced to use a special maneuver to steer around opposition in their own party and speed the measure to the floor, relying on Democratic cooperation to push it through.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The legislation pays for Department of Homeland Security operations through Sept. 30, except for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and parts of border patrol, which received an earlier influx of money from a Republican-only bill.

item.title: House Passes Farm Bill After Republican Infighting
item.description: The measure exposed rifts within the G.O.P., pitting lawmakers aligned with the Make American Healthy Again movement against those representing traditional agricultural interests.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:credit: Max Whittaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: The House approved a $390 billion measure to set the country’s agricultural policy.

item.title: How the Voting Rights Decision May Block the Rise of Young Black Leaders
item.description: Black Democrats in the South already face steep challenges when seeking political office. But the Supreme Court’s ruling could be felt for a generation.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Rory Doyle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Evan Turnage, a former congressional aide, recently challenged the veteran Democratic lawmaker Bennie Thompson in a Mississippi district vulnerable to being redrawn.

item.title: How Trump’s Iran Blockade Is Complicating a High-Stakes Trip to China
item.description: If President Trump flies to China as planned in May, the primary topic will clearly be the rippling economic effects of a war that Beijing has made clear it viewed as unnecessary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran last week showing Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz. China imports about a third of its oil and gas through the waterway.

item.title: Trump Administration Casts Host of Policies Under Biden as Anti-Christian
item.description: A new report is the latest effort by the Justice Department to argue that it is removing political bias from prosecutorial decision-making.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, vowed that the Justice Department would “continue to expose bad actors who targeted Christians.”

item.title: Chief Justice Roberts Played the Long Game on Voting Rights
item.description: Since his early days as a lawyer and in his first years on the bench, the chief justice has worked to limit the force of the Voting Rights Act.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Ron Edmonds/Associated Press
item.media:description: John Roberts and other nominees to the federal bench with President George W. Bush in 2001.

item.title: Senator Reed, a Democrat, attacks Hegseth right out of the gate.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and John Ismay
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Jack Reed, Democrat of Rhode Island, said during an Armed Services Committees hearing on Thursday that Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth had “dangerously exaggerated” the U.S. military’s accomplishments in the Iran war.

item.title: Trump Administration Imposes Caps on Graduate School Loans
item.description: The changes stem from student loan provisions in a sprawling tax and domestic policy bill signed into law last year.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officials in the Trump administration contend that reducing the amount of money that students can borrow will force universities to lower tuition.

item.title: After Supreme Court Decision, Louisiana Weighs Redrawing House Maps
item.description: The Louisiana secretary of state said the House primary election would be delayed after the court ruled the state’s congressional districts unconstitutional. The Senate primary will go forward on May 16.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry of Louisiana, a Republican, outside the White House in 2025. Mr. Landry is weighing whether to suspend the state’s May 16 primary to redraw congressional maps.

item.title: The Vaccine Skeptic in Trump’s New C.D.C. Leadership Team
item.description: Dr. Sara Brenner is a physician, an F.D.A. official and a “MAHA mom” who has said people should not reflexively believe in the benefits of vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz/Reuters
item.media:description: Dr. Sara Brenner fully embraced the Make America Healthy Again movement after being named the acting commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration, signing her first emails to the agency’s staff with the slogan.

item.title: Gov. Janet Mills Exits Maine Senate Race as an Insurgent Democrat Rises
item.description: Her withdrawal reflects the energy of the party’s left and voters’ unease with older candidates and paves the way for Graham Platner to challenge Senator Susan Collins in November.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Janet Mills of Maine was not able to match the fund-raising of Graham Platner, a progressive political newcomer, or his standing in polls of Maine Democrats.

item.title: Democrats Regret Creating Independent Redistricting Commissions After Supreme Court Ruling
item.description: A decade ago, the party sought independent redistricting commissions. Now, in an era of extreme gerrymandering, such efforts could slow Democrats as they try to keep up with Republicans.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Rachel Woolf/Associated Press
item.media:description: Colorado has an independent redistricting commission that Democrats are now trying to undo.

item.title: In Blow to Voting Rights Act, Supreme Court Embraces Claim of Racial Progress
item.description: The majority said the law was a victim of its own success and no longer needed. Dissenters responded that Congress should make the call.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: People lining up to register to vote in Selma, Ala., in February 1965.
